mn43:0.2: The Greater Series of Questions and Answers
mn43:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn43:1.3: Then Venerable Mahākoṭṭhika, emerging from seclusion in the evening, went to Venerable Sāriputta; having gone to him, he exchanged greetings with Venerable Sāriputta.
mn43:1.4: When this courteous and memorable conversation was concluded, he sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhika said this to Venerable Sāriputta:
mn43:10.1: “When the five *indriyas* have been set aside, friend, what may be known by pure mind-*viññāṇa*?”
mn43:10.2: “When the five *indriyas* have been set aside, friend, pure mind-*viññāṇa* may know the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*] as ‘space is infinite’; it may know the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*] as ‘*viññāṇa* is infinite’; it may know the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*] as ‘there is nothing.’”
mn43:11.1: “But with what, friend, does one understand a knowable *dhamma*?”
mn43:11.2: “One understands a knowable *dhamma*, friend, with the eye of discernment [*paññācakkhunā*].”
mn43:12.2: “Discernment [*paññā*], friend, has direct knowledge as its purpose, full understanding as its purpose, and abandoning as its purpose.”
mn43:13.1: “How many conditions are there, friend, for the arising of right view?”
mn43:13.2: “There are two conditions, friend, for the arising of right view—
mn43:13.3: the voice of another and appropriate attention [*yoniso manasikāro*].
mn43:13.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for the arising of right view.”
mn43:14.1: “Supported by how many factors, friend, does right view have liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] as its fruit and benefit, and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*] as its fruit and benefit?”
mn43:14.2: “Supported by five factors, friend, right view has liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] as its fruit and benefit, and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*] as its fruit and benefit.
mn43:14.3: Here, friend, right view is supported by ethical conduct, supported by learning, supported by discussion, supported by settling [*samatha*], and supported by insight [*vipassanā*].
mn43:14.4: Supported by these five factors, friend, right view has liberation of mind [*cetovimutti*] as its fruit and benefit, and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimutti*] as its fruit and benefit.”
mn43:16.1: “But how, friend, is there the production of renewed *bhava* in the future?”
mn43:16.2: “For beings, friend, obstructed by blindness [*avijjā*] and fettered by *taṇhā*, there is delighting here and there—
mn43:16.3: in this way there is the production of renewed *bhava* in the future.”
mn43:17.1: “But how, friend, is there no production of renewed *bhava* in the future?”
mn43:17.2: “With the fading of blindness [*avijjā*], friend, with the arising of knowledge, with the cessation of *taṇhā*—
mn43:17.3: in this way there is no production of renewed *bhava* in the future.”
mn43:18.2: “Here, friend, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*]—
mn43:18.3: this, friend, is called the first *jhāna*.”
mn43:19.1: “But how many factors does the first *jhāna* have, friend?”
mn43:19.3: Here, friend, for a *bhikkhu* who has entered the first *jhāna*, thought [*vitakka*] occurs, examination [*vicāra*] occurs, *pīti*, *sukha*, and one-pointedness of mind [*cittekaggatā*].
mn43:19.4: In this way, friend, the first *jhāna* has five factors.”
mn43:2.1: “‘One without discernment [*duppañño*], one without discernment [*duppañño*],’ friend, it is said.
mn43:2.2: In what way, friend, is one called without discernment [*duppañño*]?”
mn43:2.3: “‘One does not understand, one does not understand,’ friend; therefore one is called without discernment [*duppañño*].
mn43:2.4: And what does one not understand?
mn43:2.5: One does not understand, ‘This is *dukkha*’; one does not understand, ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; one does not understand, ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; one does not understand, ‘This is the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn43:2.6: ‘One does not understand, one does not understand,’ friend; therefore one is called without discernment [*duppañño*].”
mn43:2.7: “*Sādhu*, friend,” Venerable Mahākoṭṭhika said, delighting in and approving Venerable Sāriputta’s statement. Then he asked Venerable Sāriputta a further question:
mn43:20.1: “But what factors has the first *jhāna* abandoned, friend, and what factors is it endowed with?”
mn43:20.2: “The first *jhāna*, friend, has abandoned five factors and is endowed with five factors.
mn43:20.3: Here, friend, for a *bhikkhu* who has entered the first *jhāna*, sensual desire is abandoned, ill will is abandoned, dullness and drowsiness are abandoned, restlessness and remorse are abandoned, and doubt is abandoned;
mn43:20.4: thought [*vitakka*] occurs, examination [*vicāra*] occurs, *pīti*, *sukha*, and one-pointedness of mind [*cittekaggatā*].
mn43:20.5: In this way, friend, the first *jhāna* has abandoned five factors and is endowed with five factors.”
mn43:21.1: “These five *indriyas*, friend, have different domains and different fields; they do not experience one another’s field and domain, namely:
mn43:21.2: the eye-*indriya*, the ear-*indriya*, the nose-*indriya*, the tongue-*indriya*, and the body-*indriya*.
mn43:21.3: For these five *indriyas*, friend, which have different domains and different fields, which do not experience one another’s field and domain, what is their resort, and what experiences their field and domain?”
mn43:21.4: “These five *indriyas*, friend, have different domains and different fields; they do not experience one another’s field and domain, namely:
mn43:21.5: the eye-*indriya*, the ear-*indriya*, the nose-*indriya*, the tongue-*indriya*, and the body-*indriya*.
mn43:21.6: For these five *indriyas*, friend, which have different domains and different fields, which do not experience one another’s field and domain, mind is their resort, and mind experiences their field and domain.”
mn43:22.11: “Just now, friend, we understood Venerable Sāriputta’s statement in this way:
mn43:22.13: But just now, friend, we understood Venerable Sāriputta’s statement in this way:
mn43:22.15: How then, friend, should the meaning of this statement be seen?”
mn43:22.16: “Then, friend, I will make a simile for you;
mn43:22.17: for by means of a simile some wise people understand the meaning of a statement.
mn43:22.18: Just as, friend, when an oil-lamp is burning, its radiance is discerned dependent on the flame, and the flame is discerned dependent on the radiance;
mn43:22.19: so too, friend, life stands dependent on warmth, and warmth stands dependent on life.”
mn43:22.2: the eye-*indriya*, the ear-*indriya*, the nose-*indriya*, the tongue-*indriya*, and the body-*indriya*.
mn43:22.3: Dependent on what, friend, do these five *indriyas* stand?”
mn43:22.5: the eye-*indriya*, the ear-*indriya*, the nose-*indriya*, the tongue-*indriya*, and the body-*indriya*.
mn43:22.6: These five *indriyas*, friend, stand dependent on life.”
mn43:22.7: “But life, friend, stands dependent on what?”
mn43:22.9: “But warmth, friend, stands dependent on what?”
mn43:23.1: “Are those life-*saṅkhāras*, friend, the same as those *dhammas* to be felt, or are life-*saṅkhāras* one thing and *dhammas* to be felt another?”
mn43:23.2: “Those life-*saṅkhāras*, friend, are not the same as those *dhammas* to be felt.
mn43:23.3: For if those life-*saṅkhāras* were the same as those *dhammas* to be felt, then the emergence of a *bhikkhu* who has entered the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* would not be discerned.
mn43:23.4: But because, friend, life-*saṅkhāras* are one thing and *dhammas* to be felt another, the emergence of a *bhikkhu* who has entered the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* is discerned.”
mn43:24.1: “When how many *dhammas*, friend, leave this body, does this body then lie cast aside and discarded, like a senseless log?”
mn43:24.2: “When, friend, three *dhammas* leave this body—life, warmth, and *viññāṇa*—then this body lies cast aside and discarded, like a senseless log.”
mn43:25.1: “What is the difference, friend, between one who is dead and passed away and a *bhikkhu* who has entered the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*?”
mn43:25.2: “For one who is dead and passed away, friend, bodily *saṅkhāras* have ceased and settled, verbal *saṅkhāras* have ceased and settled, mental *saṅkhāras* have ceased and settled, life is exhausted, warmth has subsided, and the *indriyas* are broken down.
mn43:25.3: For a *bhikkhu* who has entered the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, bodily *saṅkhāras* have also ceased and settled, verbal *saṅkhāras* have ceased and settled, mental *saṅkhāras* have ceased and settled, but life is not exhausted, warmth has not subsided, and the *indriyas* are clear.
mn43:25.4: This, friend, is the difference between one who is dead and passed away and a *bhikkhu* who has entered the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*.”
mn43:26.1: “How many conditions are there, friend, for the attainment of liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiyā*] that is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*?”
mn43:26.2: “There are four conditions, friend, for the attainment of liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiyā*] that is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.
mn43:26.3: Here, friend, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the previous passing away of gladness and distress, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, with purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati*.
mn43:26.4: These, friend, are the four conditions for the attainment of liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiyā*] that is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.”
mn43:27.1: “How many conditions are there, friend, for the attainment of signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:27.2: “There are two conditions, friend, for the attainment of signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:27.3: non-attention to all *nimittas*, and attention to the signless element [*animittāya dhātuyā*].
mn43:27.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for the attainment of signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:27.5: “How many conditions are there, friend, for the persistence of signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:28.1: “There are three conditions, friend, for the persistence of signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:28.2: non-attention to all *nimittas*, attention to the signless element [*animittāya dhātuyā*], and previous preparation.
mn43:28.3: These, friend, are the three conditions for the persistence of signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:29.1: “How many conditions are there, friend, for emergence from signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]?”
mn43:29.2: “There are two conditions, friend, for emergence from signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*]—
mn43:29.3: attention to all *nimittas*, and non-attention to the signless element [*animittāya dhātuyā*].
mn43:29.4: These, friend, are the two conditions for emergence from signless liberation of mind [*animittāya cetovimuttiyā*].”
mn43:29.5: “This immeasurable liberation of mind, friend, this liberation of mind through nothingness, this liberation of mind through emptiness, and this signless liberation of mind—are these *dhammas* different in meaning and different in wording, or are they one in meaning, and only the wording is different?”
mn43:3.1: “‘One with discernment [*paññavā*], one with discernment [*paññavā*],’ friend, it is said.
mn43:3.2: In what way, friend, is one called with discernment [*paññavā*]?”
mn43:3.3: “‘One understands, one understands,’ friend; therefore one is called with discernment [*paññavā*].
mn43:3.5: One understands, ‘This is *dukkha*’; one understands, ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; one understands, ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; one understands, ‘This is the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn43:3.6: ‘One understands, one understands,’ friend; therefore one is called with discernment [*paññavā*].”
mn43:30.1: “This immeasurable liberation of mind, friend, this liberation of mind through nothingness, this liberation of mind through emptiness, and this signless liberation of mind—there is, friend, a method by which these *dhammas* are different in meaning and different in wording;
mn43:30.2: and there is, friend, a method by which these *dhammas* are one in meaning, and only the wording is different.
mn43:31.1: And what, friend, is the method by which these *dhammas* are different in meaning and different in wording?
mn43:31.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu* dwells pervading one direction with a mind [*cetasā*] imbued with goodwill [*mettā*], likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. Thus above, below, across, everywhere, in every way, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind [*cetasā*] imbued with goodwill [*mettā*], abundant, exalted, immeasurable, without hostility, without ill will.
mn43:31.3: With a mind [*cetasā*] imbued with compassion [*karuṇā*] …
mn43:31.4: with a mind [*cetasā*] imbued with gladness [*muditā*] …
mn43:31.5: he dwells pervading one direction with a mind [*cetasā*] imbued with equanimity [*upekkhā*], likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. Thus above, below, across, everywhere, in every way, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind [*cetasā*] imbued with equanimity [*upekkhā*], abundant, exalted, immeasurable, without hostility, without ill will.
mn43:31.6: This, friend, is called immeasurable liberation of mind.
mn43:32.1: And what, friend, is liberation of mind through nothingness?
mn43:32.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, completely going beyond the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing.’
mn43:32.3: This, friend, is called liberation of mind through nothingness.
mn43:33.1: And what, friend, is liberation of mind through emptiness?
mn43:33.2: Here, friend, a *bhikkhu*, gone to the wilderness, gone to the root of a tree, or gone to an empty hut, reflects thus:
mn43:33.3: ‘This is empty of self or of what belongs to self.’
mn43:33.4: This, friend, is called liberation of mind through emptiness.
mn43:34.1: And what, friend, is signless liberation of mind?
mn43:34.2: Here, friend, through non-attention to all *nimittas*, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in signless *samādhi* of mind [*animittaṁ cetosamādhiṁ*].
mn43:34.3: This, friend, is called signless liberation of mind.
mn43:34.4: This, friend, is the method by which these *dhammas* are different in meaning and different in wording.
mn43:35.1: And what, friend, is the method by which these *dhammas* are one in meaning, and only the wording is different?
mn43:35.2: Greed, friend, is a maker of measure; hate is a maker of measure; delusion is a maker of measure.
mn43:35.3: For a *bhikkhu* with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavassa*], they are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made unable to exist, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:35.4: As far as there are immeasurable liberations of mind, friend, the unshakable liberation of mind is declared foremost among them.
mn43:35.5: And that unshakable liberation of mind is empty of greed, empty of hate, empty of delusion.
mn43:36.1: Greed, friend, is a something; hate is a something; delusion is a something.
mn43:36.2: For a *bhikkhu* with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavassa*], they are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made unable to exist, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:36.3: As far as there are liberations of mind through nothingness, friend, the unshakable liberation of mind is declared foremost among them.
mn43:36.4: And that unshakable liberation of mind is empty of greed, empty of hate, empty of delusion.
mn43:37.1: Greed, friend, is a maker of *nimittas*; hate is a maker of *nimittas*; delusion is a maker of *nimittas*.
mn43:37.2: For a *bhikkhu* with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavassa*], they are abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made unable to exist, not liable to arise in the future.
mn43:37.3: As far as there are signless liberations of mind, friend, the unshakable liberation of mind is declared foremost among them.
mn43:37.4: And that unshakable liberation of mind is empty of greed, empty of hate, empty of delusion.
mn43:37.5: This, friend, is the method by which these *dhammas* are one in meaning, and only the wording is different.”
mn43:37.6: This is what Venerable Sāriputta said.
mn43:37.7: Satisfied, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhika delighted in Venerable Sāriputta’s statement.
mn43:37.8: The Greater Series of Questions and Answers is finished, the third.
mn43:4.2: In what way, friend, is it called *viññāṇa*?”
mn43:4.3: “‘It cognizes, it cognizes,’ friend; therefore it is called *viññāṇa*.
mn43:4.5: It cognizes *sukha*, it cognizes *dukkha*, and it cognizes neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.
mn43:4.6: ‘It cognizes, it cognizes,’ friend; therefore it is called *viññāṇa*.”
mn43:5.3: And is it possible to separate these *dhammas* out again and again and describe a difference between them?”
mn43:5.6: And it is not possible to separate these *dhammas* out again and again and describe a difference between them.
mn43:5.7: For what one understands, friend, that one cognizes; what one cognizes, that one understands.
mn43:5.8: Therefore these *dhammas* are connected, not disconnected.
mn43:5.9: And it is not possible to separate these *dhammas* out again and again and describe a difference between them.”
mn43:6.2: what is the difference between these connected *dhammas*, not disconnected?”
mn43:6.4: of these connected *dhammas*, not disconnected, discernment [*paññā*] is to be developed [*bhāvetabbā*], while *viññāṇa* is to be fully understood.
mn43:6.5: This is the difference between them.”
mn43:7.2: In what way, friend, is it called *vedanā*?”
mn43:7.3: “‘It feels, it feels,’ friend; therefore it is called *vedanā*.
mn43:7.5: It feels *sukha*, it feels *dukkha*, and it feels neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.
mn43:7.6: ‘It feels, it feels,’ friend; therefore it is called *vedanā*.”
mn43:8.2: In what way, friend, is it called *saññā*?”
mn43:8.3: “‘It recognizes, it recognizes,’ friend; therefore it is called *saññā*.
mn43:8.5: It recognizes blue, it recognizes yellow, it recognizes red, and it recognizes white.
mn43:8.6: ‘It recognizes, it recognizes,’ friend; therefore it is called *saññā*.”
mn43:9.3: And is it possible to separate these *dhammas* out again and again and describe a difference between them?”
mn43:9.6: And it is not possible to separate these *dhammas* out again and again and describe a difference between them.
mn43:9.7: For what one feels, friend, that one recognizes; what one recognizes, that one cognizes.
mn43:9.8: Therefore these *dhammas* are connected, not disconnected.
mn43:9.9: And it is not possible to separate these *dhammas* out again and again and describe a difference between them.”
